name: Sweetgrass Braid
description: Sweetgrass, known scientifically as Hierochloe odorata, is a sacred plant in many Native American cultures, particularly those of the Plains and Great Lakes regions. Its long, fragrant leaves are typically braided and used in prayer, smudging, and healing ceremonies. The sweet aroma is said to attract positive energy and spirits. In many creation stories, Sweetgrass is one of the first plants to grow on Mother Earth, making it deeply connected to creation and renewal. The braiding of sweetgrass often represents the hair of Mother Earth, symbolizing interconnectedness, harmony, and healing. Burning sweetgrass creates a cleansing smoke that purifies spaces, objects, and people, removing negative energies and preparing them for prayer or ceremony. Sweetgrass is often burned after sage to bring in positive energy after negative energies have been cleared. The plant is also used in basketry and other crafts, further demonstrating its versatility and cultural importance. The act of harvesting sweetgrass is considered a sacred practice, with prayers and offerings often given in gratitude for the plant's gifts. Respectful harvesting ensures the plant's continued growth and availability for future generations. Sweetgrass embodies peace, healing, and the sacred connection between humans and the natural world, playing a vital role in maintaining spiritual balance and well-being within Native American communities. It represents kindness, respect, and the sacredness of life.
subtitle: Sacred Herb of Purification and Healing

keyMyths.title: The First Plant
keyMyths.description: Sweetgrass is often considered one of the first plants to grow on Mother Earth. Its presence signifies the beginning of life and the abundance of the natural world.

sources.author: Robin Wall Kimmerer
sources.work: Braiding Sweetgrass: Indigenous Wisdom, Scientific Knowledge and the Teachings of Plants
sources.description: Explores the interconnectedness of humans and nature through the lens of indigenous knowledge and botany.
sources.text: Explores the interconnectedness of humans and nature through the lens of indigenous knowledge and botany.
